list: Explain when you should use the following types of pointers.

content.text: std::unique_ptr should be used when you want a smart pointer to manage a dynamic object that is not going to be shared.
std::shared_ptr should be used when you want a smart pointer to manage a dynamic object that may be shared. The object won’t be deallocated until all std::shared_ptr holding the object are destroyed.

std::weak_ptr should be used when you want access to an object that is being managed by a std::shared_ptr, but don’t want the lifetime of the std::shared_ptr to be tied to the lifetime of the std::weak_ptr.
std::auto_ptr has been deprecated and was removed in C++17. It should not be used.

list: Explain why move semantics is focused around r-values.

content.text: Because r-values are temporary, we know they are going to get destroyed after they are used. When passing or returning an r-value by value, it’s wasteful to make a copy and then destroy the original. Instead, we can simply move (steal) the r-value’s resources, which is generally more efficient.
